perturbation.original_text: within thirty (30) days after the date of termination or expiry of this Agreement, will return to the Discloser all original Confidential Information thereof then in its possession or control, (iii) if the Discloser requests in writing, within thirty (30) days after the date of termination or expiry of this Agreement, will return to the Discloser all copies of the Confidential Information requested by the Discloser, (iv) if no request from the Disclo ser has been received within thirty (30) days of the termination or expiry of this agreement for the return of any copies of the Confidential Information, will destroy all copies thereof then in its possession or control and (v) upon the written request o f the Discloser, will furnish the Discloser with written certification of such destruction .
perturbation.changed_text: within ninety (90) days after the date of termination or expiry of this Agreement, will return to the Discloser all original Confidential Information thereof then in its possession or control, (iii) if the Discloser requests in writing, within ninety (90) days after the date of termination or expiry of this Agreement, will return to the Discloser all copies of the Confidential Information requested by the Discloser, (iv) if no request from the Disclo ser has been received within ninety (90) days of the termination or expiry of this agreement for the return of any copies of the Confidential Information, will destroy all copies thereof then in its possession or control and (v) upon the written request o f the Discloser, will furnish the Discloser with written certification of such destruction .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return of confidential information to 90 days may violate British Columbia's Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) which mandates reasonable timeframes. 30 days is more reasonable than 90 in many cases and the change introduces a potential violation depending on the specifics of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) of British Columbia, Section 30(2)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 30(2) of PIPA requires organizations to return or destroy personal information when it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was collected.  Extending the period to 90 days introduces a potential violation if the information is no longer required after 30 days, as keeping it longer may be deemed unreasonable and in contravention of PIPA's principle of only retaining information as long as necessary. A 30-day window is standard, and extending it without specific justification could violate the Act.
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 90 days for returning confidential information potentially contradicts the principle of retaining information only as long as necessary, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that extending the retention period to 90 days could be unreasonable and violate PIPA if the information is no longer needed after 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1, though largely unreadable, appears to address the handling of personal information, supporting the relevance of PIPA to this contract's confidentiality clause. Because the change directly extends the time for data retention, increasing risk of non-compliance, it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality and restricted use contained herein will survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of receipt of any Confidential Information from the Discloser under this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality and restricted use contained herein will survive for a period of twenty (20) years from the date of receipt of any Confidential Information from the Discloser under this Agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 20 years could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under British Columbia law, potentially violating common law principles regarding restraint of trade, especially if the information becomes outdated or irrelevant. It's also an unusually long period for most business contexts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles Restricting Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no specific statutory limit for confidentiality agreements in British Columbia, common law principles dictate that restraints of trade (which can include overly broad or lengthy confidentiality provisions) must be reasonable. A 20-year period, especially if not narrowly tailored to specific, highly sensitive information, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint, making it unenforceable. The longer the period of restraint, the more difficult it is to justify.

Notably, the statistics indicate that 2020 saw a dramatic drop in filed charges United States Employment and HR Gerald L. Maatman Jr., Christopher DeGroff, Alex S. Oxyer +1 Authors Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Seyfarth Synopsis : The EEOC recently released its enforcement and litigation statistics for Fiscal Year 2020 . Notably, the statistics indicate that 2020 saw a dramatic drop in filed charges, with the lowest number of charges filed in over 20 years. For example, despite the #MeToo movement remaining an enforcement priority for the Commission in 2020, the number of gender discrimination charges fell even lower than last year, which was the lowest number since 1997. However, monetary benefits recovered by the Commission in FY 2020 surged. Given the flip of the White House from red to blue and the commitment of the Biden Administration to enhanced enforcement of workplace bias laws, the EEOC's enforcement data is a "must-read" for all employers. On February 26, 2021, the EEOC released its comprehensive enforcement and litigation statistics for Fiscal Year 2020 (available here ). In addition to enforcement and litigation activity, the data breaks down charge statistics by allegation and state – showing which charges are being filed and where. The dip in the number of charges that employers saw in 2018 and 2019 continued through 2020, with the number of charges reaching its lowest point since 1997. The prominence of gender discrimination charges seen in 2018 due to the #MeToo movement has all but disappeared, with sex discrimination charges remaining in the fourth-place position and dropping to their lowest number in over 20 years. Charges Were Down Overall In total, 67,448 charges were filed in FY 2020, down from 72,675 charges filed in FY 2019 and 76,418 charges that filed in FY 2018. FY 2019 previously saw the fewest charges filed for all fiscal years going back to FY 1997 (the second lowest, 75,428 charges, occurred in FY 2005). Putting these numbers in perspective, the number of charges filed exceeded 80,000 per year in every year from FY 2007 until FY 2018, sometimes by wide margins. Consistent with this overall decline, there was a decrease in almost every category of charges in FY 2020 as compared to FY 2019, with the exception of a modest increase in charges alleging claims under the Americans with Disabilities Act ("ADA"), charges alleging claims of color discrimination under Title VII, and, most nota
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period from 5 to 20 years, which the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under British Columbia common law principles. While the provided scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations argue that such a long period might be unenforceable if not narrowly tailored. The original text, with its 5-year limit, is less likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint. Thus, the changed text creates a potential legal contradiction by pushing the confidentiality period to a length that could be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Province of British Columbia and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware creates a conflict because the agreement is between two Canadian entities, one of which is located in British Columbia. Delaware law would not normally apply, raising questions of enforceability and potentially violating principles of private international law regarding choice of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Private International Law (Conflict of Laws)
perturbation.law_explanation: Private international law dictates that courts typically respect the parties' choice of law unless there is no reasonable connection to the chosen jurisdiction, or it violates public policy. In this case, with two Canadian entities and the subject matter occurring in Canada, applying Delaware law would be unusual and could be challenged on the grounds that Delaware has no substantial relationship to the agreement or the parties involved. The 'most significant relationship' test is not fulfilled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Principle VII.1 - Damages in case of non-performance | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1671 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Prof. Dr. Klaus Peter Berger, LL.M. transnational law transnational law (lex mercatoria or international business law) and "Damages in case of non-performance" 2016-03-01 13:58:16 https://www.trans-lex.org/img/logo_ball.png Document-Id: 946000, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/946000" Principle No. VII.1 - Damages in case of non-performance The aggrieved party is entitled to damages for loss caused by the other party's non-performance of its contractual obligations. It is entitled, subject to the provisions of Principle VII.2 and Principle VII.3.1 , to receive such a sum of money by way of damages as will, so far as possible, put him in the same position as if the contract had been performed. Commentary References Contract Clauses Commentary 1 The Principle, together with the Principle VII.3.1 establishes the principle of full compensation as the corner stone of the law on damages. The party suffering the damage is entitled to full compensation for the harm it has sustained as a consequence of the other party's non-performance. At the same time, the damages claimed and received must not enrich the party suffering the damage. This limitation is relevant for the recovery of future harm, including lost profits . 2 By referring to the recovery of damages "caused" by the other party's non-performance, the Principle refers to the need for a causal nexus between the non-performance and the damages. Establishing such nexus may be problematic in cases in which a party claims compensation for future damages . perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established principles of private international law by changing the governing law from British Columbia and Canadian federal law to the law of Delaware, USA. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that this is problematic because the agreement is between two Canadian entities, raising enforceability issues and violating choice of law principles as Delaware has no substantial relationship to the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that conflict of laws arises when "a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute". Applying Delaware law when the entities and subject matter are in Canada creates a conflict and undermines the predictable application of relevant Canadian laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed by facsimile or other electronic means , in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which taken together shall constitute one and the same instrument .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may only be executed by physical signature in ink, and only the original document shall be considered valid.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating electronic signatures contradicts the Electronic Transactions Act of British Columbia, which gives legal recognition to electronic signatures. This change makes the agreement more difficult to execute and contradicts established legal precedent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Electronic Transactions Act of British Columbia (ETA) explicitly states that a legal requirement for a signature is satisfied by an electronic signature if certain conditions are met. The change invalidates this provision, potentially hindering the agreement's enforceability by requiring physical signatures when electronic signatures are legally recognized and acceptable under BC law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy The Corporate, Insolvency and Competition Policy Directorate ( CIC ) is responsible for the legislative policy development and review of a number of Canada's business framework laws and regulations in the corporate, insolvency, and competition policy areas. These include the following statutes and the regulations made under them: Statutory Review of the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act and the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Comments received Consultation on the Canada Business Corporations Act Comments received Canada Not-for-profit Corporations Act Canada Business Corporations Act Canada Cooperatives Act Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Winding-up and Restructuring Act Competition Act Textile Labelling Act Consumer Packaging and Labelling Act Precious Metals Marking Act The CIC carries out its mandate by conducting policy analysis; monitoring domestic and international developments; undertaking research; consulting with stakeholders; supporting the legislative process; working to enhance public and stakeholder understanding; and, participating in international discussions and negotiations in its areas of responsibility. Report a problem Date modified: 2014-08-19
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly restricts the method of agreement execution to 'physical signature in ink' and requires the 'original document' to be valid, directly contradicting the Electronic Transactions Act, which recognizes and validates electronic signatures. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm that this change invalidates the use of electronic signatures, which are legally acceptable under British Columbia law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions corporate and insolvency law, which, while not directly related to electronic transactions, underscores the importance of adhering to established legal frameworks, something the changed text violates by disregarding the Electronic Transactions Act. The original text allows for electronic signatures, and the changed text prohibits them, therefore creating a direct contradiction.
